channel.title: Comments for page "UIU File: 2017-102"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "UIU File: 2017-102"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Speaking as someone who knows little to nothing about computers, I found this to be an interesting article and the linked SCP bit was cool too. Read it from both sides and, maybe because I was doing it on break at work, missed a few details here and there. But it links together really well, the story engaged me and the spoiler bit in the comments helped me figure out anything else I hadn't made the connections on.

A happy +1 from me.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
In my head you can handwave away WHISPER supporting every architecture set back to and including the 8086 in a few ways. PENTAGRAM is backing this and they have access to things that may make transpiling the malware trivial. They also have unlimited funding at their disposal from the US military.

Striking a balance here was tricky, what I think we had to settle for was a level where not knowing it doesn't hurt your ability to comprehend what the piece is doing, but having a background in it may give you an appreciation for the detail we put into crafting it.

I like this. Magical malware would usually be downvote material, but the way this story fleshes out the concept with real computer engineering stuff swings it into upvote territory for me.

One nitpick is with the claim that WHISPER supports "45 different instruction set architectures". Cross-platform development is expensive, especially for a program as low-level as a rootkit. Given that the majority of devices build upon a mere handful of architectures, investing in support for 45 of them seems a bit wasteful. On the other hand, this is the US military…

My main concern is that this series is quite heavy with jargon, especially on the SCP side. I guess it's unavoidable with a concept like this, but I still wonder if there's any way to make the story more approachable to non-technical readers.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Authors: pxdnbluesoul and aismallard

For Collab-Con 2019:
Unusual Incidents Unit & Pentagram

Critiquers: not_a_seagull, Uncle Nicolini

Special Thanks: Woedenaz for the UIU theme (this is it's debut!)

Full spoilers for both articles:
– hide block

The UIU has successfully delivered the mother of all malware to every device on the planet that has a processor and has ever been powered on less than 180 feet below ground. They achieve this with magic radar (WHISTLE) delivering the malware (WHISPER) over the air (WHISPER IN THE WIND, get it?) The Foundation notices this huge surge of noise (SUDDEN THUNDER) and one Chuck Thompson is put in charge of this. After determining that the UIU and Pentagram are both involved, he goes straight to the UIU looking to extort money. Chuck is dirty from the beginning and the FBI has plenty of uses for people along those lines. Chuck sells out the Foundation, and everyone, for $7.5 million. His way of handling matters is to shout down any arguments to the contrary that this is totally benign software that was distributed by mistake. It's clumsy but the procedure is that changing the classification of an SCP from Neutralized requires the approval of the lead researcher, which won't happen. John Powers is doing the same on the UIU's side by putting incriminating outbound communications under Official Review.

There's a little easter egg in the very beginning of the SCP with the image, we call attention to the public key but not the purple box below it, __imp_unpack_WHISPER.

Additionally, Chuck makes copies of all the SCP entries to his local machine so John Powers, via WHISPER, can steal them. That's what's going on in the bottom.

Pentagram developed the software and astute readers will note that the IPs referenced in the SCP belong to Pentagram, not the UIU. Pentagram receives all data acquired by WHISPER first, and then relays it to the other parties. The UIU in this case is just one user, and this is also why Pentagram is brought in the loop and provides the fixed version of ARBITER, the anomalous main piece to WHISPER, the arbiter of truth. It is patched so this specific method of being detected as laid out in the RAISA ticket won't work anymore, and that's about as far as it goes.

This was initially supposed to feature Redzone Security but they're a little too pants-on-head evil. We settled instead on two federal agencies working together, which isn't as common as you might think.
